rationale: The citing paper explicitly centers on correcting contextual distortions in conjoint responses and recovering preference shifts between stated and revealed data, while the cited paper directly studies how incentive alignment (realization probability) changes attention, processing, and partworth estimates in CBC tasks. That makes the cited work a plausible mechanism/theory source for the citing paper’s bias discussion rather than an obviously incidental mention. With only DOI-level match context available, this pair still merits second-stage review.
